---
title: "Methods"
format: html
---

## Pubmed search

We performed a PubMed search in June 2022 for the terms “United States Renal Data System” and “USRDS”, and downloaded the results.  We chose a convenience sample of the 175 most recently published papers further analysis.

PDFs of each study were obtained from the appropriate source.

## Manual review of papers
Each paper was reviewed independently by two study reviewers and the results entered into a Google Form.  The study assessment tool is below:

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Click here to see form"}

## USRDS abstraction form

### Citation information

1. **Authors**  
   Free text

2. **Title**  
   Free text

3. **Journal**  
   Free text

4. **Year**  
   Free text

### Study classification

5. **Is this paper a new data analysis?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

6. **Is this a transplant-related study?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

### Programming language

7. **Did the authors state what programming language they used?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

8. **What programming language(s) was/were used?**  

    - [ ] R
    - [ ] SAS
    - [ ] STATA
    - [ ] SPSS
    - [ ] Other: ________

### Files used

9. **Did the authors explicitly state what files they used?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

10. **What files were used?**  

    - [ ] Core
    - [ ] Transplant
    - [ ] Part D
    - [ ] Institution
    - [ ] Physician/Supplier
    - [ ] Hospital
    - [ ] Other: ________

11. **Which pieces of data were abstracted from the files?**  

    - [ ] Basic demographics
    - [ ] Residential location
    - [ ] Transplant-specific information
    - [ ] ICD9 code
    - [ ] ICD10 code
    - [ ] CPT code
    - [ ] Medications (part D)
    - [ ] Costs

12. **Does the choice of files/codes seem appropriate for the research question?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

13. **Comments**  
   Free text

### Reproducibility and funding

14. **Did the authors share the code they used?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

15. **Was the study funded?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

16. **Who funded the study?**  
   [ ] NIH

    - [ ] Foundation
    - [ ] Industry/pharma
    - [ ] Internal funding
    - [ ] Other: ________

### usRds coverage

17. **Would the major data tasks needed for this study be covered by currently proposed usRds package?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

18. **Which tasks would not be covered?**  
   Free text

### Crosswalks

19. **Were any crosswalks used?**  

    - [ ] Yes
    - [ ] No

20. **Which crosswalks were used?**  

    - [ ] UNOS
    - [ ] Physician
    - [ ] Provider

:::

## LLM review

We used an R-based pipeline built with the [`ellmer`](https://ellmer.tidyverse.org/) package to evaluate whether large language models (LLMs) could reproduce the manual review process. Each PDF was imported into R, and the full text was submitted to each of three LLMs along with a standardized set of abstraction questions modeled on the manual review form.

The `ellmer` package was used to request structured outputs, allowing model responses to be returned in a format suitable for downstream analysis in R. This made it possible to compare LLM-generated abstractions directly with human review across multiple study characteristics, including study type, transplant relevance, programming language, files used, and other extracted variables.

## Data analysis in R

Data analysis was performed in R. We first summarized the results of manual review and LLM-based extraction across the included papers, focusing on both broad study classification and more detailed methodological features. Descriptive summaries included whether a paper represented a new data analysis, whether it was transplant-related, and whether specific programming languages, USRDS files, or other analytic components were identified. These summaries provided an initial overview of model performance and helped show where the structured outputs from each LLM aligned with the human-reviewed reference standard.

### Cohen’s κ

Agreement between human reviewers and each LLM was assessed using [Cohen’s κ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cohen%27s_kappa). Cohen’s κ is a chance-corrected measure of agreement for categorical variables: unlike simple percent agreement, it accounts for the fact that some agreement would be expected even if two raters were classifying papers partly by chance. This is especially important when variables are imbalanced, such as when most papers fall into one category and only a small number fall into another. In those situations, percent agreement can appear high even when true discriminative performance is limited..

In this analysis, κ was calculated separately for each extracted variable and for each LLM compared with the human-reviewed reference set. Higher κ values indicate stronger agreement beyond chance, while lower values suggest weaker correspondence between model output and human abstraction. We interpreted κ using standard descriptive ranges, while recognizing that these categories are only rough guides and that κ can be influenced by prevalence and marginal distributions. For that reason, κ was considered alongside descriptive counts and percentages rather than in isolation.

### Heatmaps

To visualize patterns of performance across variables and models, we generated heatmaps and related summary tables. The heatmaps were designed to show where agreement clustered within groups of variables, such as analytic components, reported programming languages, or USRDS files used in a study. This approach made it easier to identify domains in which the models performed consistently well and domains in which extraction was less reliable. Together, the descriptive summaries, κ statistics, and heatmaps provided a complementary view of model performance, combining overall agreement metrics with more detailed inspection of where errors occurred.
